title: Small Mason Bee House
summary: A small wall-mounted mason bee house with an angled roof and a removable, replaceable drilled nesting block.
description:
Most "bee hotel" plans go big — one large box with hundreds of holes for every solitary bee species at once. This one goes the other way on purpose: small, single-species-focused, and built around a nesting block you're expected to swap out, not one you build in and forget.

The **nesting block is not glued in.** Solitary bee larvae overwinter inside the holes, and old, unreplaced nesting material builds up mites and mold over a few seasons — a fixed block turns a helpful habitat into a health hazard for the bees using it. Keeping it removable is the entire point of building your own instead of buying a sealed one.

Paint the exterior only, in blue or purple (bees see these well; avoid red and dark tones), and leave the nesting wood itself bare.

tools.note: Cuts the 22.5° bevels on the roof and side panels.
Gives cleaner, flatter-bottomed nesting holes than a standard twist bit.

materials.name: Exterior spray paint (blue or purple)
materials.note: Avoid red and dark tones. Exterior only — leave the nesting wood bare.

cutList.note: 22.5° bevel on the top edge so the two panels meet at a peak.
Top edge beveled to match the roof angle.
Drilled with 5/16" (or slightly larger) holes, 6-8" deep. Removable, not glued in — swap every 1-2 years.

steps.title: Cut the roof and side panels
steps.body: Cut the two roof panels with a 22.5° bevel on the top edge so they'll meet at a peak, and the two side panels with the top edge beveled to match.

steps.title: Cut the bottom and back panels
steps.body: Cut a bottom panel and a back panel from scrap stock or plywood.

steps.title: Assemble the box
steps.body: Glue the sides, bottom, and back together into a box, then attach the two beveled roof panels on top so they meet at the peak.

steps.title: Build the nesting block
steps.body:
Laminate scrap 2x4 stock into a block sized to the depth of the housing, then drill 5/16" (or slightly larger) holes 6-8" deep to serve as the nesting insert.

Vary the hole diameter and depth across the block rather than standardizing — different bee species and sexes use different sizes, so a mix attracts more occupants than one uniform size.

steps.title: Paint and finish
steps.body: Paint the exterior only, in blue or purple — avoid red and dark tones. Leave the nesting block completely unfinished.

steps.title: Mount and maintain
steps.body: Mount with the roof overhang facing morning sun exposure, keeping the nesting holes sheltered from rain. Replace the nesting block every 1-2 years — this is regular maintenance, not a repair.
